front: Figure: traditionally believed to have been transferred as an embryo into the womb of Queen Trishala by the commander of Indra's army (a Svetambara belief)
Figure: son of King Siddhartha of the Ikshvaku dynasty's Jnatr/Gyat clan; some traditions hold he was a prince of Kosala
Figure: attained kevalajnana (omniscience) at age 72, becoming the 24th tirthankara
Figure: his teachings were divided into twelve Angas and fourteen Purvas at the Council of Pataliputra after centuries of oral transmission
Figure: traditionally married to Yashoda, with whom he had a daughter
Figure: a temple at Pavapuri contains footmarks said to be his
Figure: this class of 24 teacher-figures' name means "ford-makers," for creating a passage across samsara; only the last two are historically attested
Figure: the "five auspicious events," or Panch Kalyanaka, occur in each of their lives, including renunciation and the attainment of absolute knowledge
Figure: the god Indra presides over these five events and appears as a white elephant in the first auspicious dream announcing one's conception
Figure: because their images lack distinctive features, they are identified by lanchhana, individual symbols
Figure: first of the 24 tirthankaras, legendarily said to have lived for roughly 600 quintillion years
Figure: attained kevala jnana while meditating under a banyan tree
Figure: father of Bahubali and Bharata; the pilgrimage site Palitana is dedicated to him, whom he visited 99 times in life
Figure: Svetambara Jains undertake the alternating-day varshitapa fast in his honor; he broke his own fast by accepting sugarcane juice from his great-grandson King Sreyamsa
Figure: the 23rd and penultimate (second-to-last) tirthankara, preceding Mahavira
Figure: per Svetambara tradition, founded the doctrine of "fourfold restraint" and was the only tirthankara to gain the title Kalikalkalpataru
Figure: Svetambaras believe this 19th tirthankara, the "lord of jasmine," was female; Digambaras believe all tirthankaras were male
Figure: the dispute over this 19th tirthankara's gender underlies the largest Jain schism, over whether women can attain liberation without being reborn male
Figure: his teacher Bhadrabahu convinced him his conquests would cause a twelve-year famine, leading him to abdicate his throne to Bindusara and become a Jain
Figure: allegedly starved himself to death in a cave in Karnataka, following the Digambara tradition
Denomination: "sky-clad" sect whose ascetics go naked, keeping only a water gourd and a peacock-feather broom to sweep away insects
Denomination: holds that women cannot attain moksha without first being reborn male, and that all 24 tirthankaras, including Mallinatha, were male
Denomination: believes its scriptures (the Agamas) have gradually disappeared, so the surviving Angas are not authentic
Denomination: central pilgrimage town Shravanabelagola contains the 57-foot Gommateshwara statue, ritually bathed in milk every 12 years
Denomination: the Adipurana, an epic by a member of this sect, recounts the life of Bahubali, credited as the first to achieve enlightenment in the present age
Denomination: according to its own tradition, founded by the 1st-century monk Sivabhuti after a dispute over whether a monk could keep a gifted shawl
Denomination: a 20th-century revival movement founded by Kanji Swami distinguished the "true pure knowledge self" from "the other" as a path to enlightenment
Denomination: philosophers Kundakunda and Pujyapada, of this sect, developed theories of the "pure soul" and wrote a major commentary on the Tattvartha Sutra
Denomination: "white-clad" sect whose monks wear white robes, contrasted with naked Digambara monks
Denomination: believes Mahavira's embryo was transferred into Trishala's womb by the commander of Indra's army
Denomination: temples often include brightly painted statues of tirthankaras, unlike largely unpainted Digambara temples
Denomination: recognizes five categories of false belief (mithyatva), while Digambaras recognize seven
Denomination: Sthanakavasi is a 15th-century iconoclastic reform movement within Svetambara Jainism; Terapanth is its 18th-century offshoot
Denomination: these two Svetambara reform sects refuse to worship murtis (temple statues); Jains who continue such worship are called "murti-pujaka"
Denomination: Acharya Tulsi founded the Anuvrat movement within Terapanth to universalize Jainism's lesser vows after leading a WWII peace campaign
Practice: monks sweep the ground in front of them with a broom (such as the rajoharana or a peacock-feather whisk) to avoid stepping on and killing insects
Practice: monks may wear cloth masks over their mouths to avoid inhaling and harming small creatures
Practice: this vow leads many adherents to practice vegetarianism
Practice: symbolized by an open hand (hamsa) with a dharmachakra wheel inside it
Practice: ritual fasting to death, undertaken voluntarily; in 2015 India's Supreme Court overturned a Rajasthan ban after ruling it is not suicide
Practice: a supplementary/optional vow taken in addition to the five main vows
Practice: its most extreme form, Padapopagamana, requires the practitioner to stand motionless "like a tree" until death
Practice: a meditative practice of stillness aimed at equanimity, typically lasting 48 minutes for laypeople
Practice: often performed after samayika, this ritual of asking forgiveness ("micchami dukkadam") is repeated as often as twice a day or as rarely as once a year, during Paryushana
Doctrine: holds that truth is "many-sided" and no single viewpoint captures it completely; illustrated by a parable of blind men who each perceive an elephant differently
Doctrine: uses the qualifier "syad" ("maybe") to develop a sevenfold logical scheme, e.g. "in some ways, it is, it is not, and it is indescribable"
Doctrine: this epistemology was shared with the atomist Vaisheshika and Ajivika schools; Umasvati was a major scholar of it
Doctrine: karma is conceived as physical particles that "influx" (asrava) onto the soul through bodily activity and are then "bound" (bandha) to it
work: Karma, Jiva, and the Nine Tattvas

front: Doctrine: metaphysics recognizes nine fundamental principles (navatattva), including jiva (soul) and ajiva (non-living matter)
work: Karma, Jiva, and the Nine Tattvas

front: Doctrine: the soul (jiva) is classified into five types by how many senses its possessing organism has, from one-sensed beings like plants up to five-sensed beings
work: Karma, Jiva, and the Nine Tattvas

front: Doctrine: bad behavior causes particle accumulation on the soul unless one becomes a kevalin, an omniscient liberated being
work: Karma, Jiva, and the Nine Tattvas

front: Doctrine: four kasaya ("faults") — anger, pride, deceit (maya), and greed — impede reaching right belief (samyaktva), alongside mithyatva and nidana
work: Karma, Jiva, and the Nine Tattvas

front: Doctrine: the Five Great Vows (Mahavratas) are ahimsa, satya (truth), asteya (non-stealing), brahmacharya (celibacy), and aparigraha (non-possession)
Doctrine: this vow of "non-stealing" is violated by acts like using short weights/measures or intentionally buying something below a fair price
Doctrine: laypeople take reduced vows called Anuvratas, alongside further categories called Gunavratas and Siksavratas
Doctrine: right conduct (following the vows), right knowledge, and right faith together form this "Three Jewels" path to moksha
Doctrine: time is divided into six recurring periods; the current period is duhsama, during which human height and lifespan progressively shrink
Doctrine: the distant past is said to have included "wishing trees" that fulfilled needs when asked
Doctrine: this mountain is the center of the universe in Jain (and Buddhist) cosmology; Indra carries newborn tirthankaras there
Doctrine: this building models the terrestrial world as described in Jain cosmology
Doctrine: nigodas are beings possessing only the sense of touch
Doctrine: pilgrimage sites called tirthas ("fords") are physical locations where tirthankaras achieved moksha; devotees may substitute study of tirth pat maps for physical pilgrimage
Text: this term denotes the orally-transmitted teachings of Mahavira, a term also used by various Tantric traditions
Text: Digambaras and Svetambaras dispute whether the original versions of these scriptures were completely lost in a famine following Chandragupta Maurya's conquests
Text: the Upasaka Dashanga Sutra, a Svetambara agama, governs the laity and calls them sravaka/sravika
Text: written by Umasvati (Umaswati), the only text considered authoritative by both the Digambara and Svetambara sects
Text: contains the aphorism "souls render service to one another," using the term jiva for living matter
Text: the most important Jain prayer, recited while bowing to the Panch Parameshthi ("Supreme Five"), which includes arihants and siddhas
Text: opens by stating "I bow to [these people]" — bowing to arihants, of whom tirthankaras are a subgroup, distinguished from the less accessible siddhas
Text: the word "namo" in this prayer refers to the act of bowing/prostration, also performed while reciting the Khamasamana Sutra
Holiday: a major annual holiday preceded by intensified fasting practice
Holiday: the pratikraman ritual of asking forgiveness ("micchami dukkadam") is performed during this holiday, as often as twice daily or as rarely as once yearly
Religion: its name derives from a Sanskrit word meaning "to conquer," reflecting its focus on self-control
Religion: one of the Sramana movements, alongside Buddhism, which rejected Vedic tradition
Religion: in 1974, adopted an official emblem with three dots near the top and a dot inside a curve, representing a liberated soul
Religion: wealthy adherent merchant families such as Shantidas Jhaveri and the Jagat Seth family served the Mughals and the Nawabs of Bengal
Religion: a Gujarati group of adherents who migrated from Palanpur came to control Antwerp's diamond trade, previously run by the Hasidic community
Religion: in ancient South Asian sources, adherents were referred to by a term meaning "free from knots"
Religion: the earliest known image made by adherents, held at the Patna Museum, dates to approximately the 3rd century BCE
Religion: stone-carved temples at this mountain are a prominent pilgrimage site
Religion: medieval stories from this faith were collected in The Forest of Thieves and the Magic Garden by Yale scholar Phyllis Granoff
